Why is Jerusalem in such terror? Her slain men are not slain with the sword, but with famine; or, they are slain with fear, disheartened. Their rulers fled, but were overtaken. Those who serve God, who foresee and warn sinners of coming miseries, are affected by the prospect. But all the horrors of a city taken by storm faintly show forth the terrors of the day of wrath.

The weakness of Judah now appeared more than ever. They also discovered their reliance on physical things and their physical security. They looked to the fortifications. They made sure of water for the city. But they disregarded God in all these preparations. They did not care for his glory in what they did. They did not depend upon him for a blessing on their efforts. For every creature is what God makes it to be; and we must bless him for it, and use it for him. There was great contempt of God's wrath and justice in contending with them. God’s design was to humble them and bring them to repentance. They walked contrary to this. Actual disbelief of another life after this is at the bottom of the reliance on physical things and brutish sensuality, which are the sin, shame, and ruin of so great a part of mankind. God was displeased at this. It is a sin against the remedy, and it is not likely they will ever repent of it. Whether this unbelief works by presumption or despair, it produces the same contempt of God, and is a token that a person will perish willfully.

This message to Shebna is a reproof of his pride, vanity, and security; what vanity is all earthly grandeur, which death will so soon end! What will it avail whether we are laid in a magnificent tomb or covered with the green sod? Those who, when in power, mistreat others, will be justly mistreated themselves. Eliakim should be put into Shebna's place. Those called to places of trust and power should seek God for grace to enable them to do their duty. Eliakim’s advancement is described. Our Lord Jesus describes his own power as Mediator, that he has the key of David. His power in the kingdom of heaven, and in ordering all the affairs of that kingdom, is absolute. Rulers should be fathers to those under their government; and the honor men bring unto their families by their piety and usefulness is more to be valued than what they derive from them by their names and titles. The glory of this world gives a person no real worth or excellence; it is but hung upon him, and it will soon drop from him. Eliakim was compared to a nail in a sure place; all his family are said to depend upon him. In eastern houses, rows of large spikes were built up in the walls. Upon these moveables and utensils were hung. Our Lord Jesus is as a nail in a sure place. That soul cannot perish, nor that concern fall to the ground, which is by faith hung upon Christ. He will set before the believer an open door, which no man can shut, and bring both body and soul to eternal glory. But those who neglect so great salvation will find that when he shuts, none can open, whether it be shutting out from heaven or shutting up in hell forever.
